dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 responding, report to --Address Redacted-- for breathing problem.
Hi, I'm Sarah, an EMT with the rescue squad. How can we help you today?
I'm having a lot of abdominal pain and shortness of breath. It just started getting really bad.
How long have you been feeling this way?
About 30 minutes now. It was manageable before, but now it feels like 10 out of 10.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name?
Jane.
Well done, Jane. What day is it today?
Thursday? Let me think... Thursday or Wednesday?
Okay, and what county are we in?
[CITY]? I think we are in [STATE].
Okay, and what happened to you tonight?
I just started having terrible belly pain again, and I felt like I couldn't catch my breath.
Jane, is your vision clear and working as usual?
Yes, my vision is fine.
Can you explain a little more about your pain?
I had this pain last night too. It was a 6 out of 10, but it went away after I slept.
Jane, can you raise your arms for me?
Sure.
Great job, Jane. Now, can you move your legs as well?
Yes, I can.
Good work. Thanks for your help.
Have you had any signs of trauma or distress?
No, nothing like that.
Do you have any past medical history I should know about?
I have GERD.
What medications are you currently taking?
I take Zantac and Zofran.
Any medication allergies?
Nope, no allergies.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - event leading up to complaint
dialogue.utterance: Was there anything else you noticed before the pain started, like what you did or how you slept?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - event leading up to complaint
dialogue.utterance: I had trouble sleeping and then woke up with this awful pain. I thought I could sleep through it, but it came back worse.
When exactly did the pain first start?
Around 8 PM.
Can you describe the pain - like sharp?
It feels like cramps, but all over my belly.
Does it move or stay in one place?
It stays in my belly.
On a scale of 0 to 10, where 10 is the worst ever, how bad is your pain now?
We are going to do a quick check of your breathing. Can you take a deep breath for me?
Okay.
Breath sounds are clear in all fields. No signs of decreased air exchange or noises in your chest that worry me right now.
Pulse is 74 and regular. Your hands are warm and pink. Good signs of circulation so far.
Blood pressure is 129 over 81. That's acceptable at this time. Let me check your sweating or signs of pale skin - no signs noted, so that's good too.
Glucose is 148. That's within normal range for now. Anything here that raises concern?
Respirations are 22 per minute. Let's continue with the rest of our check.
Jane, you are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. GCS is 15. No signs of neurological concerns at the moment. Good job articulating with us.
We're going to put on 4 lead ECG monitoring now to assess for any cardiac-related cause for your shortness of breath.
CV - ECG - 12 Lead Obtained. All leads are working and giving us baseline normal sinus rhythm for now. Nothing acute on board.
Jane, next we're going to start a line for IV fluids. We'll put 200 cc saline in slowly. Any allergies to medications?
No.
We will use aseptic techniques for the IV in your left arm. Our procedures are in line with Statewide Orders guideline. I'll be using a fresh glove set, wipe site, secure a tourniquet.
Done successfully. IV line established in an extremity vein. We'll watch for any swelling or redness at the site while on route.
Protocol applied: Medical - Abdominal Pain (Protocol 3-2). We're following this because of high pain and generalized concern of GI activity.
Pulse is still 74, regular. Respirations have decreased slightly to 20 per minute.
Blood pressure is 118 over 82. Still normal for us. Nothing critical there. Let's check what's going on with your breath now.
Spo2 is 98% and stable. No concerns for now. Your breathing remains comfortable on our 4-point check across your lungs.
EKG shows sinus rhythm and is normal. Nothing to stop us from proceeding.
Fluid is being administered slowly and without issue. Can you feel any discomfort at the IV site?
How is the pain now? Still experiencing it or any relief?
Still 10. Worse now actually - the movement isn't helping much.
Head and neck look normal. No signs of swelling or trauma. No masses noted.
I'll gently press on parts of your abdomen now for a quick check.
That hurts - everywhere. Including the left and right side.
We'll avoid further pressure, just checking. You're showing some guarding, which is normal in the setting of pain. No rigidity noted.
Skin looks normal, no bruising or rashes. No dryness or thinness suggesting dehydration.
Let's take another look at your breathing. Right now, respirations are 18 per minute. That's within the normal range.
Oxygen saturation is now at 100%. Still nice and comfortable. No change in work of breathing or signs of distress here.
Blood pressure is 118 over 77. Heart is still strong. Pulse steady. Everything's consistent.
Jane, based on our assessment, we believe the best place for you is [COMPANY_NAME]. Is there any problem with us taking you there?
No, go ahead.
We will carefully load you onto the stretcher. Let me use the sliding board to assist you smoothly into position.
Ready to transport Jane in semi-Fowler's position. You should be more comfortable here with your breathing in that way.
The abdomen is soft with no distention. Let's secure the restraints to ensure your safety during transport.
Jane secured with straps. Clear for transport. Please stay quiet if you can to avoid pain aggravation.
We will be transporting you cold today due to your condition. Supply is on board and administered as per protocol.
En route to [COMPANY_NAME]. ETA is about 12 minutes from our current position. Nothing here changes our decision.
Let me check once more if Jane's IV is running without restrictions. All signs of IV-place well. No redness or pain from the arm.
Jane, how is the pain now? Is there anything we can adjust for comfort before handoff?
It's still a 10. Movement isn't helping. I'll just try to stay still.
Final assessment vitals: Pulse 73, Respirations 18, Blood Pressure 118/77. Oxygen saturation is at 100%. Consistently stable, as per ePCR.
Care will be transferred upon arrival. All systems maintained and TOC documented.
All current medications given en route: normal saline according to the ePCR timeline and protocol.
Chief complaint: abnormal abdominal pain, 10/10, with shortness of breath in a 23-year-old female. Primary concern remains GI-based but cardiac ruled out with 12-lead performed.
History of GERD, on Zantac and Zofran. Unremarkable allergies.
Vitals trended and stable en route per timeline in the ePCR as well as in this assessment. Blood pressure dipping slightly but, per protocol, still within framework to continue monitoring.
Patient is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. GCS 15. Pain remains high, but otherwise she is following us well and retaining spoken information during transport.
Handoff now complete to receiving ED without incident. [COMPANY_NAME] has been alerted about abdominal pain with shortness of breath en route per patient verbalization and progress.
Transport completed. Ambulance is sitting at the ramp with a full code. Receiving ED updated en route and we are prepared to assist if transfer to stretcher becomes necessary.
Final vitals pre-handoff: pulse 73, respirations 18, blood pressure 118/77, oxygen saturation at 100%, and EKG in sinus rhythm. All remain consistent.
Wound dressing is dry and clean. IV flow remains patent and at proper speed from EMT prior briefing notes.
Optimal lighting and emergency meds in compliance with in-transit monitoring. Everything stable prior to transfer.
TOC handed over according to general EMS protocol without incident. [COMPANY_NAME] accepted with confirmation of pain status.
No medical emergencies or protocol failures along this transport. [COMPANY_NAME] staff reported ready for Jane.
Complete documentation update. All steps recorded prior to transfer. Chain of care remains unbroken under standard practice.
[COMPANY_NAME] obtained with no incidents during lakeside transport. Jane is well enough for ED workup.
No pain increase or respiratory compromise at the last marker. All interventions effective and repeated as needed.
Patient ready for safe handoff. All care to [COMPANY_NAME] is complete. Thank you for your cooperation, Jane. You're safe now.
All systems and interventions stable. Pass SAMPL and OQRST details to receiving RN now. Serial EKGs and abdominal pain remain prioritized until ED takes over.
Thanks for your cooperation, Jane. You'll be in good hands at the hospital. If you feel anything change, let me know right away.
